I just watched Predator: Badlands, and honestly, it hit me in a strange way. The way the Predator moves—the expressions, the subtle moments—it feels like they’re trying to humanize him. It’s no longer just about a creature hunting humans. It’s almost like a family drama, a story about a son trying to earn his father’s approval.

But here’s the twist—this father isn’t the kind to pat you on the back. If his son disobeys him, he’ll kill him. No hesitation. That dynamic changes everything. It turns what used to be a simple monster movie into something more psychological, more emotional—even disturbing in a way.

For decades, we’ve seen the Predator as the ultimate villain. The ugly, terrifying creature who hunts humans for sport. The same one that faced off against Arnold Schwarzenegger in the jungle. We loved the gadgets, the thermal vision, the plasma cannon, the sleek armor—everything about him screamed “cool” but deadly.

But now, in Badlands, it’s like watching a wrestling storyline where the heel suddenly turns babyface. It’s like when Roman Reigns flipped the script and became the fan favorite. That’s exactly what’s happening here—the Predator, the monster we once feared, is slowly being turned into the protagonist.

And it’s not just this movie. Hollywood has been doing this for years. They’re running out of ideas, so they start flipping villains into misunderstood antiheroes. Look at Joker—the movie turned one of the most iconic villains into a tragic, sympathetic figure. Then you’ve got Maleficent, where Disney took the evil queen and gave her a soft, human backstory.

It’s like we’re entering an era where every bad guy needs a reason, every monster needs a heart. What’s next? Michael Myers getting therapy? Freddy Krueger having a redemption arc? Jason Voorhees saving kids instead of hunting them? Maybe even Ghostface or Pennywise will end up cracking jokes and showing they’ve “changed.”

Hollywood seems obsessed with the “bad-but-cool” formula. They’re trying to make the villain relatable, funny, and somehow morally complex. And sure, it works for some people—it’s entertaining. But to me, it feels like they’ve lost the essence of what made those villains iconic in the first place.

Even Chucky has gone through it. The old Chucky was pure chaos—evil with no filter. But now, in the TV series, he’s killing people while being witty, quirky, even self-aware. It’s part horror, part comedy, part weird social commentary. It’s entertaining, but it’s not the same.

And now the roles are reversed on the hero side too. Superman—the symbol of goodness and hope—has been twisted into darker versions. We’ve got Homelander in The Boys, Omni-Man in Invincible, and even alternate evil Superman storylines. Hollywood’s playing both sides: making villains good, and heroes bad.

It’s like wrestling storytelling now—constant heel turns and face turns. One moment your hero is pure, the next he’s broken and dangerous. One moment your villain is a monster, the next he’s a tragic legend.

Maybe that’s just how Hollywood works today. Maybe audiences want that gray area—the blurred line between good and evil. But for me, it feels like something’s missing. I miss when the bad guys were truly bad, and the good guys stood for something real. I miss when we didn’t have to sympathize with the monster to appreciate the story.

Still, Predator: Badlands is interesting in its own way. It made me think about how much storytelling has changed. Maybe the real predator now isn’t on the screen—it’s Hollywood itself, hunting for anything left to twist, flip, and remake.

Badlands: When Predator Becomes the Hero — Wait, What?!

I grew up watching Predator (1987) — that jungle, that roar, and Arnold covered in mud outsmarting the ultimate hunter. 💪🔥
Decades later, Hollywood drops Badlands, and suddenly the Predator’s nephew is the hero?! His partner — a human woman?!
Like we must leave the Earth if she’s a real woman 😅
From the moment Arnold yelled “Get to the chopper!” to Terminator 2 in 1992, the man defined heroism. Now, 30 years later, the legend’s shadow meets redemption, betrayal, and… romance?
Nostalgia meets madness. Welcome to Badlands — where the hunter becomes hunted, and even aliens fall in love. 💀💔🌍

And it definitely takes away the mystique, which can never be put back on the bottle. 

Predators have spoken before, in short mimicked phrases. We definitely don’t need to see them communicating compete with subtitles. Loved Prey. The recent Predator animated series was also good, although also a bit outside the bounds of classic predator. You know how Alien works best as horror with hard sci fi elements? Predator is thriller with horror/action elements, and hints of sci fi. Once you make it fully sci-fi (we dive into their world), it’s no longer a proper thriller. I remember seeing a spoof-like demotivational poster of eight pieces of silverware, one of which was bent and twisted and totally useless. In large bold it proclaimed, "Unique." And in smaller letters it said, "just because you are unique, does not mean you are useful." That's kind of what I think about when I see this trailer. I don't WANT to think like that, because I do appreciate that the filmmakers are trying something a bit different here. But, the Predator franchise, for me anyways, has always been at its best when it lets us peek into their mythology without seeing it as a whole. It gives is a quick tease, but leaves the rest to our imagination. Similar to the way Mad Max: Fury Road had so many bread crumbs about the structure of its world, but never fully divulged what it was about. I hope this is a good movie and that people enjoy it, but I don't know that I'll go see it. "Prey" for whatever faults it might have had, was a really cool approach to the Predator franchise. It wasn't a classic, but it was solidly entertaining and even has rewatch value. This trailer, though it looks cool, strips the Predator of its mystery and aura. Maybe that's for you, and if it is, have fun, I hope you enjoy the movie. I think I prefer some mystery. My $.02.
